CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE OF JUN-06-2018 CONTINUED TO AUG-01-2018 AT 10:30 AM IN DEPARTMENT 610. NOTICE SENT BY COURT.

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT FORD MOTOR COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO THE FIFTH AND SEVENTH CAUSES OF ACTION IN THE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT PLAINTIFF FILED BY PLAINTIFF PATRICK FLOYD IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO FIFTH CAUSE OF ACTION FOR BREACH OF THE IMPLIED WARRANTY OF MERCHANTABILITY AND SUSTAINED WITH TWENTY DAYS LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO SEVENTH CAUSE OF ACTION FOR FRAUD BY OMISSION. COUNSEL FOR FORD IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE COURT'S RULING IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN, CLERK: M.GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR#8600, TORREANOM@AOL.COM, REPORTED. =(302/HK)

REPLY TO PLAINTIFFS OPPOSITION TO DEFENDANTS MOTION FOR A MANDATORY TRANSFER OF VENUE (TRANSACTION ID # 61466031) FILED BY DEFENDANT FORD MOTOR COMPANY

LAW AND MOTION, DEPT. 302, DEFENDANT FORD MOTOR COMPANY'S MOTION TO TRANSFER VENUE TO NAPA COUNTY IS DENIED. MR. FLOYD'S CHOICE OF VENUE IS PRESUMPTIVELY CORRECT AND FORD FAILS TO MEET ITS BURDEN OF DEMONSTRATING THAT SAN FRANCISCO IS AN IMPROPER VENUE. FORD RELIES ON THE DECLARATION OF COUNSEL AND A HEARSAY CARFAX REPORT TO SHOW THAT CCP 395(B) VENUE IS IMPROPER. NONE OF THAT EVIDENCE IS ADMISSIBLE MOREOVER, AT MOST, THE COURT COULD TRANSFER THIS ACTION TO FORD'S PRINCIPAL PLACE OF BUSINESS IN CALIFORNIA PER BEUTKE V. AMERICAN SECURITIES CO. (1955) 132 CAL.APP.2D 354, 361-362. THAT WOULD BE SANTA CLARA COUNTY, NOT NAPA COUNTY. AS INDICATED IN FONTAINE V. SUPERIOR COURT (2009) 175 CAL. APP. 4TH 830, CCP 395(B) WAS INTENDED TO PROVIDE BROADER RIGHTS TO A CONSUMER AND NOT RESTRICT THOSE RIGHTS AS FORD SEEKS TO DO BY ARGUING THAT THE BEUTKE RULE IS INAPPLICABLE TO SECTION 395(B). (AS FULLY REPORTED IN THE REPORTER'S TRANSCRIPT). ORDER IS SIGNED. (SEE SCANNED ORDER FOR DETAILS). JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN, CLERK: M. GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: MELANIE GHENO, CSR# 7489, MGHENO@SFTC.ORG, REPORTED. (D302/HK)
MINI MINUTES FOR DEC-19-2017 09:30 AM FOR DEPT 302

LAW AND MOTION, 302, DEFENDANT FORD MOTOR COMPANY DEMURRER TO THE FIFTH AND SEVENTH CAUSES OF ACTION OF PLAINTIFF'S UNVERIFIED COMPLAINT FOR VIOLATION OF STATUTORY OBLIGATIONS ORDERED OFF CALENDAR TO GIVE THE PARTIES AN OPPORTUNITY TO COMPLY WITH CCP 430.41. THE MAY DECLARATION SAYS ONE BASIS FOR A DEMURRER WAS DISCUSSED WITH OPPOSITION COUNSEL "BY PHONE," BUT MULTIPLE OTHER GROUNDS WERE NOT. PLAINTIFF'S COUNSEL IS ORDERED TO COMPLY WITH CCP 430.41 BY PROPERLY MEETING AND CONFERRING; FORD NEED NOT ANSWER THE COMPLAINT UNTIL AFTER THERE IS COMPLIANCE. JUDGE: RICHARD B. ULMER JR., CLERK: M. GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR#8600, TORREANOM@AOL.COM, REPORTED. (D302/RBU)

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-06-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jessica Zamora, Esq. of Strategic Law Practices, 310.929.4900, appearing by CourtCall for the Plaintiff;
Richard J.. May, Esq. of Schnader Harrison Segal & Lewis LLP, 415.364.6727, appearing for the Defendant.

DEFENDANT FORD MOTOR COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO THE SIXTH CAUSE OF ACTION OF PLAINTIFF'S UNVERIFIED SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT FOR VIOLATION OF STATUTORY OBLIGATIONS.

Matter called, ARGUMENT held.

Ruling: At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling as modified:
Defendant Ford Motor Company's demurrer to the sixth cause of action for fraud by omission in the second amended complaint filed by plaintiff Patrick Floyd is sustained without leave to amend. Mr. Floyd was previously given leave to amend to plead specific facts showing his delayed discovery of Ford's alleged fraud. "In order to rely on the discovery rule for delayed accrual of a cause of action, [a] plaintiff whose complaint shows on its face that his claim would be barred without the benefit of the discovery rule must specifically plead facts to show (1) the time and manner of discovery and (2) the inability to have made earlier discovery despite reasonable diligence. In assessing the sufficiency of the allegations of delayed discovery, the court places the burden on the plaintiff to show diligence; conclusory allegations will not withstand demurrer." (Fox v. Ethicon Endo-Surgery, Inc. (2005) 35 Cal.4th 797, 808 (citation and internal quotation marks omitted)). As explained in Cansino v. Bank of America (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 1462, 1472: "Because the discovery rule operates as an exception to the statute of limitations, if an action is brought more than three years after commission of the fraud, plaintiff has the burden of pleading and proving that he did not make the discovery until within three years prior to the filing of his complaint. To excuse failure to discover the fraud within three years after its commission, a plaintiff also must plead facts showing that he was not negligent in failing to make the discovery sooner and that he had no actual or presumptive knowledge of facts sufficient to put him on inquiry. To that end, a plaintiff must allege facts showing the time and surrounding circumstances of the discovery and what the discovery was. Conclusory allegations will not withstand a demurrer. The discovery related facts should be pleaded in detail to allow the court to determine whether the fraud should have been discovered sooner. (Citations and internal quotation marks omitted). The statute of limitations for a fraud claim is 3 years per CCP 338. Mr. Floyd's allegations show that he was on inquiry notice by early 2013 and he filed his complaint on September 6, 2017. The defects in the car existed at the time of the sale and began manifesting themselves in 2013. (Second Amended Complaint pars. 9 and 48.) Mr. Floyd's allegation at paragraph 50(c) where he states that he discovered the fraud in April of 2017 at the time of the buyback is conclusory. Mr. Floyd does not allege what specifically gave him reason to suspect fraud at that time rather than in 2014. Mr. Floyd's new allegations at paragraph 9 merely recount the car's repair history and, if anything, those allegations show that he had inquiry notice by January of 2013 as a matter of law. Counsel for Ford is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling as modified with regard to the date of inquiry notice as corrected on the record by Judge Kahn (they inquiry notice date was corrected to 2013). The proposed order is to be in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR 8600, 415.551.5868, Email: torreanom@aol.com. =(302/HEK)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jul-13-2018 Continued To Oct-03-18 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-12-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Carey Wood, Esq. 310-929-4900 of Strategic Legal Practices for Plaintiff Patrick Floyd through Court Call.
Richard May, Esq. 415-364-6700 Schnader Harrison Segal & Lewis for Defendant FORD MOTOR COMPANY.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT FORD MOTOR COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO THE FIFTH AND SEVENTH CAUSES OF ACTION OF PLAINTIFF'S UNVERIFIED 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT FOR VIOLATION OF STATUTORY OBLIGATIONS.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Defendant Ford Motor Company's demurrer to the fifth and seventh causes of action in the first amended complaint plaintiff filed by plaintiff Patrick Floyd is sustained without leave to amend as to fifth cause of action for breach of the implied warranty of merchantability and sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to seventh cause of action for fraud by omission. The implied warranty claim is barred by the four year statute of limitations. Commercial Code 2725(2) provides that: "A cause of action accrues when the breach occurs, regardless of the aggrieved party's lack of knowledge of the breach. A breach of warranty occurs when tender of delivery is made, except that where a warranty explicitly extends to future performance of the goods and discovery of the breach must await the time of such performance the cause of action accrues when the breach is or should have been discovered." Mr. Floyd alleges that he purchased the vehicle in September of 2012 and he filed his action in September 2017. For the statute of limitations for the implied warranty claim, the breach occurred when Mr. Floyd purchased the vehicle, i.e., when the tender of delivery happened. The accrual exception based on discovery does not apply to the implied warranty claim. The future performance exception "applies only when the seller has expressly agreed to warrant its product for a specific and defined period of time." (Cardinal Health 301, Inc. v. Tyco Electronics Corp. (2008) 169 Cal.App.4th 116, 130 (emphasis in original)). The Cardinal Health court explained that "[b]ecause an implied warranty is one that arises by operation of law rather than by an express agreement of the parties, courts have consistently held [that] it is not a warranty that explicitly extends to future performance of the goods." (Id. at 134.) Mr. Floyd is given leave to amend his fraud claim to allege with more particularity the delayed discovery of the alleged fraud. (Hobart v. Hobart Estate Co. (1945) 26 Cal.2d 412, 437; Fox v. Ethicon Endo-Surgery, Inc. (2005) 35 Cal.4th 797, 808.) Ford's other arguments are rejected. While Bigler-Engler v. Breg, Inc. (2017) 7 Cal.App.5th 276, 310-311 supports Ford's position that Mr. Floyd does not plead a transaction to support the concealment claim, the weight of authority indicates that such a transaction is not necessary under the instant circumstances. (Jones v. Conocophillips (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 1187, 1199; OCM Principal Opportunities Fund v. CIBC World Markets Corp. (2007) 157 Cal.App.4th 836, 859; Reniger v. Hyandai Motor America (2015) 122 F.Supp.3d 888, 896.) Paragraphs 47 and 50 of the amended complaint sufficiently allege Ford's "exclusive knowledge" to support the concealment claim. If Mr. Floyd is able to allege a timely concealment claim, the economic loss rule is inapplicable.

Counsel for Ford is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M.Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. =(302/HK)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Mandatory Transfer Of Venue
Off Calendar Dec-19-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Anna Knafo, Esq. 310-425-3431 of Strategic Legal Practices for Plaintiff through Court Call.
Richard May, Esq. 415-364-6727 of Schnader Harrison Segal & Lewis LLP for Defendant through Court Call.

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues, cites page 838, reads Fontaine case. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes)The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT FORD MOTOR COMPANY's Motion For A Mandatory Transfer Of Venue.

Defendant Ford Motor Company's motion to transfer venue to Napa County is denied. Mr. Floyd's choice of venue is presumptively correct and Ford fails to meet its burden of demonstrating that San Francisco is an improper venue. Ford relies on the declaration of counsel and a hearsay Carfax report to show that CCP 395(b) venue is improper. None of that evidence is admissible Moreover, at most, the court could transfer this action to Ford's principal place of business in California per Beutke v. American Securities Co. (1955) 132 Cal.App.2d 354, 361-362. That would be Santa Clara County, not Napa County. As indicated in Fontaine v. Superior Court (2009) 175 Cal. App. 4th 830, CCP 395(b) was intended to provide broader rights to a consumer and not restrict those rights as Ford seeks to do by arguing that the Beutke rule is inapplicable to section 395(b). (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Melanie Gheno, CSR# 7489, mgheno@sftc.org, Reported. (D302/HK)
